cases.transcript: Pencil. The pencil is mine. Tree. The tree is oak. Now I'm going to give you two words. I'd like you to use both of them for me in a short sentence. Child, hospital. The child is in the hospital. Cold, winter. It is a cold winter day. Now I'm going to give you three words. I'd like you to use them all for me in a sentence. Chair, doctor, sit. The doctor sit in the chair. Bureau, open, drawer. The bureau drawer is open.
cases.patient_only_transcript: The pencil is mine. The tree is oak. The child is in the hospital. It is a cold winter day. The doctor sit in the chair. The bureau drawer is open.
speaker_turn_summary: Examiner: 'Pencil. The pencil is mine.'
Patient: 'The pencil is mine.'
Examiner: 'Tree. The tree is oak.'
Patient: 'The tree is oak.'
Examiner: 'Now I'm going to give you two words... Child, hospital.'
Patient: 'The child is in the hospital.'
Examiner: 'Cold, winter.'
Patient: 'It is a cold winter day.'
Examiner: 'Now I'm going to give you three words... Chair, doctor, sit.'
Patient: 'The doctor sit in the chair.'
Examiner: 'Bureau, open, drawer.'
Patient: 'The bureau drawer is open.'
patient_cue_summary: Task-compliant sentence generation with full lexical inclusion
No hesitation, repetition, or filler words
Grammatical and semantic coherence across all prompts
Stable fluency and syntactic control
No evidence of retrieval failure or task breakdown

content_findings.label: sentence_construction_task_response
content_findings.summary: Patient generates grammatically correct, task-compliant sentences using provided word sets without hesitation or error.
content_findings.evidence: Patient responds to 'child, hospital' with 'The child is in the hospital.' To 'cold, winter' with 'It is a cold winter day.' To 'chair, doctor, sit' with 'The doctor sit in the chair.' To 'bureau, open, drawer' with 'The bureau drawer is open.' All responses are complete, syntactically structured, and semantically coherent.

content_findings.label: lexical_retrieval_and_task_adherence
content_findings.summary: Patient accurately retrieves and uses all target words in context without prompting or correction.
content_findings.evidence: Patient correctly incorporates all three or four target words into each sentence without omission or substitution, demonstrating intact lexical access and task comprehension.

content_findings.label: fluency_and_coherence
content_findings.summary: Responses are fluent, self-contained, and demonstrate stable syntactic and semantic organization across multiple sentence tasks.
content_findings.evidence: No pauses, fillers, repetitions, or self-corrections observed. Each sentence is delivered in a single, coherent utterance with appropriate subject-verb-object structure.

assessment.screening_summary: Patient demonstrates intact sentence construction, lexical retrieval, and task adherence across multiple structured language prompts. No impairment-like cues observed in speech or behavior. No evidence of cognitive decline in this clip.
evidence_against_risk: All responses are grammatically correct and semantically complete.
No hesitation, repetition, or filler words observed.
Task compliance is consistent across all prompts without examiner scaffolding.
alternative_explanations: Normal language performance in a structured screening task.
No signs of retrieval failure, disorganization, or task breakdown.
risk_control_suggestions: Continue routine cognitive screening as part of standard care.
No further clinical evaluation indicated based on this clip.
assessment.visit_recommendation: routine follow-up or standard screening as appropriate for patient age and clinical context.
assessment.future_risk_trend_summary: Longitudinal prediction not possible from single clip; no evidence of cognitive decline in this assessment.
context_notes: video not available but not impacting usability.
Patient clearly responds to examiner prompts without assistance or error.
Task performance is consistent with healthy cognitive function in structured language tasks.
assessment.disclaimer: Research demo only. This output is not a diagnosis and must not be used as a standalone medical decision.

cases.transcript: A trellis. Trellis, okay. And you can begin now. Apples, pears, peaches, celery, turnips, meat, bread, candy, pickles, tomatoes, potatoes, celery, turnips, bananas, oranges, plums, buns, peas, beans, cantaloupes. Okay, good. I'm going to change directions, but you can begin now. Saw, see, say, soothe, suck, sample, sort, see, sympathize, summer, sail, sail, snub, snort, snicker, slop, slang, slide, slow, sleuth, sustain, sample, seething, slug, sound. Okay, good. Very good.
cases.patient_only_transcript: Apples, pears, peaches, celery, turnips, meat, bread, candy, pickles, tomatoes, potatoes, celery, turnips, bananas, oranges, plums, buns, peas, beans, cantaloupes. Saw, see, say, soothe, suck, sample, sort, see, sympathize, summer, sail, sail, snub, snort, snicker, slop, slang, slide, slow, sleuth, sustain, sample, seething, slug, sound.
speaker_turn_summary: Examiner: 'A trellis. Trellis, okay. And you can begin now.'
Patient: 'Apples, pears, peaches, celery, turnips, meat, bread, candy, pickles, tomatoes, potatoes, celery, turnips, bananas, oranges, plums, buns, peas, beans, cantaloupes.'
Examiner: 'Okay, good. I'm going to change directions, but you can begin now.'
Patient: 'Saw, see, say, soothe, suck, sample, sort, see, sympathize, summer, sail, sail, snub, snort, snicker, slop, slang, slide, slow, sleuth, sustain, sample, seething, slug, sound.'
Examiner: 'Okay, good. Very good.'
patient_cue_summary: Patient delivers a long, uninterrupted list of items in two fluency tasks without hesitation or correction.
Patient repeats 'celery, turnips' in the first list, indicating mild repetition but not significant retrieval failure.
Patient transitions smoothly to a second fluency task with 'saw, see, say...' without prompting or pause.
Patient maintains task relevance and lexical productivity in both tasks, with no evidence of semantic drift, tangentiality, or breakdown.
Patient does not exhibit pauses, fillers, self-corrections, or signs of retrieval difficulty in either fluency task.
Examiner provides minimal scaffolding and does not need to correct or prompt the patient during either task.

assessment.screening_summary: Patient demonstrates adequate verbal fluency in two structured tasks, with no observable signs of retrieval difficulty, semantic disorganization, or task breakdown. Mild repetition of items occurs but does not indicate impairment. Examiner provides minimal scaffolding, suggesting patient is largely self-sufficient. No emotional or behavioral cues suggest distress or apathy. Audio quality is low, and visual data is unavailable, limiting confidence in full assessment.
evidence_against_risk: Patient completes both fluency tasks without pauses, fillers, or corrections.
Patient maintains task relevance and lexical productivity in both categories.
No evidence of semantic drift, tangentiality, or retrieval failure.
Examiner does not need to prompt or correct the patient during either task.
alternative_explanations: Low audio quality may mask subtle impairments not detectable in this clip.
Patient may be fatigued or distracted, but this is not evident from the speech content.
The tasks may be too easy for the patient to demonstrate impairment, even if present.
risk_control_suggestions: Consider repeating the fluency tasks with more challenging categories or time constraints.
Add a memory or executive function task to complement fluency assessment.
If available, obtain a visual or behavioral observation to supplement this audio-only assessment.
assessment.visit_recommendation: Routine follow-up with formal cognitive screening if symptoms persist or progress.
assessment.future_risk_trend_summary: Longitudinal prediction is limited based on a single audio-only clip with no visual or behavioral data. Further assessment is recommended if clinical concerns arise.
context_notes: Audio-only assessment with low quality and no visual data.
Patient appears to complete both fluency tasks without difficulty.
Examiner provides minimal scaffolding, suggesting patient independence.
No signs of dementia-like impairment are evident in this clip.
assessment.disclaimer: Research demo only. This output is not a diagnosis and must not be used as a standalone medical decision.
